coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and visual probes for sensing copper ions in living cells via an electron transfer process
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jian
Sheng Li, Rong
Zhi Zhang, Hong
Wang, Ni
Zhang, Zheng
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: As an integral part of many important enzymes, Cu2+ is involved in a number of vital biological processes, which is linked to the oxidative damage and environmental contamination when Cu2+ is excessive. In this work, Cu2+ can be captured by the amino groups of carbon dots (CDs) to form complexes, resulting in a strong fluorescence quenching of CDs via a nonradiative electron transfer process, which offered a rapid, visual, and selective methodology for Cu2+ detection. The probe exhibited a wide response concentration range (0.01–2μM) to Cu2+ with a detection limit of 6.7nM. Significantly, the CDs presented excellent biocompatibility and high photostability, which were applicable for the visualization of Cu2+ dynamic invasion into living cells and Tilapia mossambica. Furthermore, the toxicity of Cu2+ ions to living cells could be inhibited with CDs by the formation of complexes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Copper
Electron transfer
Cellular imaging
Visual sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Standard procedure for detecting Cu2+ ion
Fluorescence response of carbon dots towards Cu2+
Possible mechanism of the fluorescence response of carbon dots to Cu2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optical and morphological characterization of the as-prepared CDs. (A) UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs. a, UV–Vis spectrum; b, excitation spectrum; c, emission spectrum; inset, optical images under daylight (left) and 365nm UV light (right). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 480nm at the interval of 20nm. (C) TEM image of CDs. a, HRTEM images of CDs; b, HRTEM image with lattice parameters of 0.34nm; c, The electron diffraction pattern; d, size distribution of CDs. (D) AFM image and height distribution of CDs.

The sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ detection. A, The fluorescence response of CDs (50μgmL-1) in the presence of increasing concentration of Cu2+(from 0 to 2μM). Inset: the standard curve for Cu2+ detection. B, The selectivity test. 0, Cu2+, 2μM; 1–20 were 20μM metal ions including Fe3+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Sr2+, Pb2+, Na+, Mg2+, Li+, K+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Ag+. 21–32 were 20mM anions including F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, SO4 2- NO3 -, PO4 3-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, S2-. 33–55 were 200mM biomolecules, including Histidine, isoleucine, valine, threonine, serine, asparagine, tryptophan, proline, leucine, tyrosine, lysine, arginine, glutamine, glutamic acid, glycine, alanine, methionine, cysteine, glutathione and bovine serum albumin, vitamin C, glucose and lecithin.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism investigation. A, fluorescence lifetime change of CDs caused by Cu2+; B, Zeta potential change; C, cyclic voltammetry; D, energy level simulation; E, the schematic description of the electron transfer process.

Laser confocal microscopic images and quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity of HEp-2 cells incubated with CDs and different concentrations of Cu2+ during various periods. c Cu2+, a, 0μM; b, 1μM; c, 5μM; d, 10μM; e, 50μM. a1-e1, 0min; a2-e2, 20min; a3-e3, 40min; a4-e4, 60min; a5-e5, 80min.

The imaging of Tilapia mossambica. a, the bright field imaging of Tilapia mossambica incubated with CDs for 4h; b, the fluorescent imaging of Tilapia mossambica incubated with CDs for 4h; c, the bright field imaging of Tilapia mossambica incubated with CDs for 4h and then with copper for 2h; d, the fluorescent imaging of Tilapia mossambica incubated with CDs for 4 h and then with copper for 2 h; concentration of CDs 0.5mg/mL; concentration of copper, 50μM.
The scheme diagram for determination of copper ions in living cells.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and visual probes for sensing copper ions in living cells via an electron transfer process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an integral part of many important enzymes, Cu2+ is involved in a number of vital biological processes, which is linked to the oxidative damage and environmental contamination when Cu2+ is excessive. In this work, Cu2+ can be captured by the amino groups of carbon dots (CDs) to form complexes, resulting in a strong fluorescence quenching of CDs via a nonradiative electron transfer process, which offered a rapid, visual, and selective methodology for Cu2+ detection. The probe exhibited a wide response concentration range (0.01–2μM) to Cu2+ with a detection limit of 6.7nM. Significantly, the CDs presented excellent biocompatibility and high photostability, which were applicable for the visualization of Cu2+ dynamic invasion into living cells and Tilapia mossambica. Furthermore, the toxicity of Cu2+ ions to living cells could be inhibited with CDs by the formation of complexes.

ce:section.ce:para: As the third most abundant transition metal in the human body, Cu2+ serves as an essential cofactor for a variety of enzymes, including tyrosinase, Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase, cytochrome c oxidase, and so on (Gaetke and Chow, 2003; Halliwell and Gutteridge, 1984). However, when copper is excessive in human body, its redox property would harm the biological system due to its ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), which participates in the cellular metabolism (Flemming and Trevors, 1989; Linder and Hazegh-Azam, 1996). Sophisticated regulatory mechanisms are available to maintain the important balance between toxicity and necessity of Cu2+ at cellular level (Rae et al., 1999). Therefore, misregulation of Cu2+ homeostasis in cells is also connected to many diseases such as cancers (Brady et al., 2014), neurodegenerative Parkinson's, Alzheimer's and Huntington's diseases (Barnham and Bush, 2014), and genetic disorders including Menkes and Wilson's diseases (Kaler, 2011). However, many questions about the trafficking mechanisms and quantification of Cu2+ in living cells remain unanswered.

Optical imaging with fluorescent probes has been an efficient approach for answering questions about how Cu2+ functions in biological systems (Ding et al., 2014; Que et al., 2008). However, fluorescent dyes are easy to photo-bleach (Zhu et al., 2016) and conventional quantum dots are limited for the well-known cell toxicity (Hardman, 2006). Carbon dots (CDs), which generally refer to carbon nanomaterials less than 10nm in size, are proposed to be promising substitutes of fluorescent dyes and quantum dots for intracellular sensing and imaging since CDs exhibit many advantages, such as excellent biocompatibility, good photostability, low toxicity, etc (Fong et al., 2016; Gupta et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2014; Yuan et al., 2015). Plenty of researchers have made great efforts to the detection of copper ions with CDs (Dong et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2012; Qu et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012; Zong et al., 2014), however, some CDs require further functionalization or doping, resulting in a more complicate strategy for Cu2+ analysis. Dong et al. developed a fluorescent probe of polyamine-functionalized CDs for sensitive and selective detection of copper via inner filter effect, which was applicable to the analysis of copper ions in water samples (Dong et al., 2012).

Herein, a kind of CDs for the specific detection of Cu2+ were obtained by carbonizing poly-ethylenimine (PEI) with microwave irradiation. These CDs present high stability, good biocompatibility, and high intracellular delivery efficiency. Furthermore, the as-prepared CDs are highly specific to Cu2+ even if present in the complicated environment, such as cell medium or biological organism. It has been proved that the photo-induced electron transfer happened from the inner core of CDs to the coordination complexes of Cu2+ and amino groups on the surface of CDs, thus Cu2+ would prevent the direct electron-hole recombination of CDs, resulting the fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the obtained CDs could be employed for imaging and monitoring the cellular activities of Cu2+ in living cells. Hence, CDs may have great potential as a high-performance platform for real-time tracking of Cu2+ in living cells. Particularly, the toxicity of Cu2+ ions towards living cells could be inhibited in some degree with CDs by forming the complexes ( Scheme 1).

Poly(ethylenimine) (PEI, Mw=600) was obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Doubly distilled water was used throughout this work.

The absorption and fluorescence spectra were scanned with a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) and a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. A LabRAM HR800 laser confocal Raman spectrometer was to record the Raman spectrum at room temperature on an Ag substrate. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CDs were obtained with a Shimadzu FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA) was performed with an accelerating voltage at 200kV for CDs imaging. The thickness distribution of CDs was measured with a multimode atomic force microscopy (AFM). Zeta potential and size distribution were carried out on a Nanosizer (ZEN3600, Malvern). A C11347 Absolute PL Quantum Yield Spectrometer (Hamamatsu, Japan) supplied the absolute quantum yield of CDs. In addition, a FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France) provided the fluorescence lifetime of CDs. Fluorescence images of cells and Tilapia mossambica were operated on a DSU live-cell confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan) system with laser excitations of DAPI (CLSM). The redox property of CDs was examined by cyclic voltammetry with a CHI600E electrochemical workstation. A Galanz microwave oven (Guangdong, China) was applied to prepare CDs.

The fluorescent CDs were prepared through a simple microwave synthetic route. Briefly, 2mL PEI was placed into a 10mL beaker, and then was directly heated with a Galanz microwave oven at 280W for 30min. The resultant product was then adjusted the acidity to neutral with 1M HCl. After the purification with BIOSHARP membrane filters (0.22µm), the obtained transparent aqueous solution was then subjected to dialysis (Mw=1000Da) for 24h to eliminate the unreacted residue. Finally, the yellow solution was dried by freeze-drying under vacuum (−80°C) to obtain PEI-CDs, which was dissolved with water and stored at 4°C for further application.

To compare with PEI-CDs, citric acid (CA)-CDs were prepare with the similar method just replace 2mL PEI with 1g citric acid monohydrate. Unless otherwise specified, CDs used in this work refer to PEI-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard procedure for detecting Cu2+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Cu2+ was performed at room temperature. In a typical assay, 0.10mL BR buffer (pH 7.0) and 0.10mL CDs aqueous solution (500μg/mL) were pipetted into a 2.0mL tube, followed by the addition of Cu2+ at different concentrations. Then, blended well and diluted to 1.00mL with water, and the samples were mixed again and then transferred to scan the photoluminescence spectra on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The selectivity for Cu2+ detection was tested in a similar way, just instead of Cu2+ with other ions.

The cell viability was evaluated through a MTT assay. Briefly, 100μL 1×105 cells/mL of human epidermoid (HEp-2) cancer cells in Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 (RPMI 1640) medium supplemented with 2% fetal bovine serum were incubated in a 96-well plate for 24h at 37°C. And then, the culture medium of cells was instead of the mixture of 90μL of RPMI 1640 medium and 10μL PEI-CDs at different doses (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25mg/mL), which was incubated at 37°C for another 24h. Finally, 10μL Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) solution and 90μL RPMI 1640 medium were pipetted into each well, which were followed by further incubation for 1h. The optical density (OD) of the mixture was obtained with a Microplate Reader Model at 450nm. The cell viability was estimated according to the equation in our previous works (Yuan et al., 2015, 2016).

For the cytotoxicity investigation of copper ions, HEp-2 cells were incubated with the mixture of 90μL of RPMI 1640 medium and 10μL Cu2+ at different doses (0, 10, 50, 100, and 500μM) for 30min, which were washed twice with PBS buffer and then cultured in RPMI 1640 medium for 24h. Finally, the cell viability was evaluated with CCK-8 solution as described above. To assess the cytotoxicity of the CDs-Cu2+ mixture, CDs at the same concentration (0.2mg/mL) were firstly incubated with cells for 24h, then treated with 10μL Cu2+ at different doses (0, 10, 50, 100, and 500μM) for 30min. After that, cells were washed twice with PBS buffer, and then cell cytotoxicity was evaluated with CCK-8 solution as described above.

To test the biocompatibility towards living fish, Tilapia mossambica were hatched at 25°C for 4 days, and changed water every day. And then, every 100 Tilapia mossambica per group were incubated with CDs at different doses (0, 0.5, 1.5, and 2.5mg/mL) at 25°C for 4h or 24h. Then, CDs solution was replaced with water to find the number of dead fish. The fish viability was estimated according to the following equation:
Viability =
100 − dead
fish
100

300μL HEp-2 cells (1×105 cells/mL) were planted to a 24-well plate in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 2% fetal bovine serum, which were then cultured in an incubator (37°C, 5% CO2) for 24h. After that, cells were cultured with 270μL RPMI 1640 medium and 30μL CDs (2mg/mL) for 4h, which were rinsed with the RPMI 1640 medium for three times to remove the remaining CDs. After the treatment with 30μL Cu2+ at different doses (0, 10, 50, 100, and 500μM), cells were then mounted with glycerol on microscope slides for the images that were acquired by a confocal microscope.

The imaging of Tilapia mossambica was achieved by a different way. Wherein, Tilapia mossambica were cultured in an incubator (25°C) for 4 days. Then, Tilapia mossambica were cultured with 0.5mg/mL CDs for 4h and then incubated with copper for 2h. The imaging of Tilapia mossambica focused on the tail because Tilapia mossambica were much larger than cells and they swam back and forth.

The remarkable properties of the as-obtained CDs are confirmed by the optical spectra and electron microscopes. The resultant CDs present characteristic absorption at 248 and 344nm, which are ascribed to the large amount of π-electrons in the sp2-hybridized structure and the n-π* transition of CDs (Yuan et al., 2015, 2016), respectively. Corresponding, two prominent peaks at about 1356cm−1 and 1606cm−1 present in Raman spectrum (Fig. S1), which are attributed to the disordered D-band of sp3 carbons and the crystalline G-band of sp2 carbons. Moreover, CDs display narrow and symmetrical fluorescence spectra profile at 462nm when excited at 355nm with the quantum yield of 26.7%, which is competent to act as a highly sensitive energy donor. In addition, the brown-yellow CDs exhibit strong blue luminescence under the irradiation of 365nm UV light lamp ( Fig. 1A). The excitation-dependent emission (EDE) phenomenon could be observed from 450nm to 550nm when excited form 280nm to 480nm at the interval of 20nm (Fig. 1B), which possibly corresponds to the distribution of different sizes and distinctive surface states (Yuan et al., 2016).

The as-obtained CDs are well water-soluble due to the hydrophilic groups such as COO-, -NH and -OH. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S2), the absorption bands at 2920cm−1 and 1647cm−1 are attributed to C–H and N-H bending vibrations, respectively. In addition, the absorption band at 3444cm−1 suggests the presence of -NH and/or -OH groups, which is propitious to improve the hydrophilicity of CDs solution.

The morphology of CDs is presented in HRTEM (Fig. 1 C) and AFM (Fig. 1 D). The as-obtained CDs are well monodispersed and uniform in size. The space distance is 0.34nm, which should be attributed to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon (Hou et al., 2016). Two sets of diffraction rings are observed, which corresponds to the basal plane spacing and in-plane lattice spacing, respectively (Li et al., 2014b). As statistics in the TEM image, the diameters of CDs mainly present in the narrow distribution range of 4.0–5.2nm with an average size of 4.6nm (Fig. 1C). The AFM image in Fig. 1 D shows an average height of the obtained CDs about 6.9nm, which may be concluded that CDs are monodisperse nanospheres.

Additionally, the fluorescence was decreased in the pH range from 2 to 4 (Fig. S3A), and remained unchanged in the pH range from 5 to 12. This pH-dependent emission feature of CDs is mainly relative with the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on CDs surface (Wu et al., 2014). Importantly, the as-prepared CDs did not show any change when CDs were dispersed in salty solution even if the concentration of NaCl as high as 2M (Fig. S3B), suggesting the high stability of CDs in high ionic strength medium, which is applicable to biochemical applications in cells. The biocompatibility of CDs is very important for the further biomedical applications, which was assessed by the CKK-8 assay.

In addition, it has been found that CDs could be uptaken by Tilapia mossambica. It displayed very weak blue auto-fluorescence (b and c in Fig. S6). However, after the incubation with CDs it presented very bright blue fluorescence around antisternum (Fig. S6e). Then incubated with water for 2h, the bright blue fluorescence was slightly weakened by the metabolism (Fig. S6f). When the concentration of CDs was lower than 2.5mg/mL, the viability of Tilapia mossambica was higher than 97% when incubated with CDs for 24h, suggesting good biocompatibility of CDs (Fig. S7 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of carbon dots towards Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the feasibility of CDs for Cu2+ detection was explored. In the absorption spectra, the presence of Cu2+ results in the great red-shift compared with the absorption spectrum of CDs, which may be attributed to the formation of complex between amino groups and Cu2+(Dong et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012). In the fluorescence spectra, Cu2+ leads to the remarkable reduce both in the excitation and emission intensity of CDs. This quenching can be directly observed from the imaging under UV light irradiation (Fig. S8). In addition, the FTIR spectrum of the CDs-Cu2+ system displays that the stretching vibration and bending vibration of N-H blue shifted by 46.3cm−1 and 19.52cm−1 with respect to that of the initial CDs, respectively (Fig. S2). However, the C-H stretching vibration at 2920.23cm−1 does not show any shift, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching may be partly attributed to the amino coordination with Cu2+.

Fig. 2 displays the fluorescence spectra of the CDs solution after incubating with various concentrations of Cu2+, which present a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity at 462nm, suggesting that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was sensitive to Cu2+ concentrations. The quenching efficiency ((I 0-I)/I 0) displays a good linear relationship versus the concentrations of Cu2+ in the range of 0.01–2μM (the insert in Fig. 2 A), where I 0 and I are fluorescence intensities at 462nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. The limit of detection is estimated to be 6.7nM (3σ/k), which is comparable to those obtained by other fluorescent probes for Cu2+(Dong et al., 2012). In addition, the reaction completes within 1min (Fig. S9) and could not be affected by the temperature (Fig. S10). This observation suggests that CDs with quick response could be employed as fluorescent probe for real-time tracking of Cu2+ ions in the biological system.

The complexity of the intracellular system poses a great challenge to the analytical methods for metal ion detection not only in sensitivity but more importantly in selectivity. Thus, the selectivity test was carried out before cellular application. As shown in Fig. 2B, no obvious interferences were detectable for other representative cations and anions. Taking into account that amino acids and proteins in the biological system were capable of interacting with plenty of metal ions, several typical amino acids and proteins were also examined as potential interferences, which caused the negligible fluorescence quenching. All these results clearly demonstrated that CDs could act as the fluorescence probes for highly selective analysis of Cu2+ over other biological related species, which could meet the selective requirements for bioimaging applications.

The high sensitivity and selectivity for copper ion may benefit from the amino group from PEI-CDs. To confirm this point, CA-CDs were prepared with the average size of 4.2nm (Fig. S11) and emission at 452nm, which possessed the low quantum yield (~7.1%). Importantly, CA-CDs presented very weak response towards copper ions. No obvious fluorescence quenching was observed when CA-CDs mixed with 2μM Cu2+. However, the same concentration of Cu 2+ led to a great degree of quenching of PEI-CDs, suggesting that the amino group plays very important role for capturing copper (Fig. S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the fluorescence response of carbon dots to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: CDs possess a variety of functional groups and optical properties, which could bind targets through distinctive mechanisms, such as inner filter effect (Dong et al., 2012), aggregation induced emission quenching (Zhao et al., 2011), or electron transfer (Chandra et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2017), etc. In this work, the possible mechanism was investigated by fluorescence lifetime, Zeta potential, cyclic voltammetry and energy level simulation.

Fig. 3A shows that the fluorescence lifetime of CDs decreases from 5.45ns to 2.92ns when in the presence of Cu2+, suggesting the occurrence of a dynamics quenching (Li et al., 2014a). The fluorescence quenching eliminates the possibility of the aggregation of CDs because neither their sizes (Fig. S13) nor their light scattering signals (Fig. S14) were much increased when Cu2+ is present, the reason lies in the fact that both of them are positively charged (Fig. 3B). Moreover, Cu2+ leads to the zeta potential decrease from 39.83mV to 11.73mV, which is attributed to the formation of the complex (Dong et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012) to inhibit the protonation of amino groups on the surface of CDs.

Inspired by previous reports that amino groups exhibited good binding affinity for Cu2+(Lin et al., 2011), we propose a non-radiative electron-transfer mechanism for the Cu2+ mediated fluorescence quenching of CDs. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular (LUMO) energy levels of CDs could be estimated according to the empirical formula (Liang et al., 2006; Min et al., 2010):
E
LOMO
= − e
(
E
Red
+ 4.4
)
E
HOMO
= − e
(
E
Ox
+ 4.4
)
E
HOMO
=
E
LOMO
−
E
g
Where E Ox and E Red are the onset of oxidation and reduction potential for CDs, respectively. The E Red is determined to be −0.32eV (Fig. 3C), and the corresponding E LUMO is calculated to be −0.48eV. The HOMO energy could not be obtained due to the irreversible of the oxidation behaviour. To determine the HOMO levels, we combine E Red with the optical energy band gap (Eg, resulting from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum), which is estimated to be 3.82eV. As previously reported, the crystal field stabilization energy (CFSE) of the complex was 1.98eV (Bossu et al., 1977). When Cu2+ is present, the complexation between Cu2+ and amino groups of CDs leads to the splitting of d orbital of Cu2+. Therefore, electrons in the excited state of CDs have an opportunity to transfer electron to the d orbital of Cu2+. Electron transition in the radiation form (fluorescence emission) of CDs is consequently restrained, leading to fluorescence quenching (Fig. 3D), as in the case of other carbon dots (Zhang et al., 2014). Thus, the mechanism of Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs is described in Fig. 3E. When Cu2+ is present, the complexation between Cu2+ and amino groups of CDs is formed to initiate the electron transfer to quenching the fluorescence of CDs.

Much different from our proposed electron transfer mechanism, Dong et al. found that the polyamine-functionalized CDs were applied to the sensitive and selective detection of copper via an inner filter effect (Dong et al., 2012), which resulted from the particular carbon source (citric acid and branched poly(ethylenimine)) and synthetic approach (pyrolyzing at 200°C for 3h), leading to the overlap between the absorption band and the excitation spectrum as well as the emission spectrum to occur the inner filter effect.

As mentioned above, CDs have advantages of small size, high selectivity, good photostability, and especially biocompatibility, substantially making them superior in potential cellular applications. Hence, HEp-2 cells were chosen as a model cell line to further demonstrate the availability of CDs for Cu2+ sensing, since other cell lines, such as PC12, HeLa, A549 and HepG2, displayed the similar results with HEp-2 cell line (Fig. S15).

As displayed in Fig. 4, after the incubation of HEp-2 cells with CDs for 4h at 37°C, a significant blue emission from the intracellular region could be observed in the cytoplasm, suggesting that CDs successfully and efficiently entered cells via endocytosis. A further investigation of different periods (0, 20, 40, 60, 80min) revealed that the fluorescence of CDs hardly decreased (Fig. 4, a1-a5), suggesting that CDs were highly stable against the photobleaching, which are an ideal candidate for long-term imaging monitoring of the intracellular activities of Cu2+ in HEp-2 cells (Fig. 4).

Then, exogenous Cu2+ at different concentrations was introduced to the CDs-pretreated HEp-2 cells to further investigate the application of CDs in semiquantitative monitoring of the intracellular Cu2+ level. When the concentration of Cu2+ was as low as 1μM, the fluorescence rarely decreased, which was possibly attributed to that a small amount of Cu2+ went into cells (Fig. 4, b1-b5). When Cu2+ increased to 5μM, the great quenching of CDs fluorescence could be observed within 80min (Fig. 4, c1-c5). If Cu2+ reached to 10μM, the emission of CDs was substantially quenched within 40min (Fig. 4, d1-d5). The total disappearance of CDs fluorescence could be achieved within 40min when the concentration of Cu2+ increased to 50μM (Fig. 4, e1-e5), indicating that the higher concentration of Cu2+ resulted in a faster quenching of CDs. The above observation revealed that PEI-CDs possess capacity of highly sensitive visualization and monitoring the activity of Cu2+ in living cells. Herein, the sensing speed and sensitivity was much lower than that in aqueous solution possibly lies in the fact that both the outer and inner membrane exhibited high Cu2+ adsorption capacity (Wang et al., 2004).

A great deal of in vitro and cell culture studies are largely supportive of Cu's capacity of initiating oxidative damage (Flemming and Trevors, 1989; Linder and Hazegh-Azam, 1996), which is in good agreement of our cell viability investigation (Fig. S16). The cell viabilities deceased to 79%, 54%, 14% and 11% when the HEp-2 cells were in the presence of Cu2+ at the concentration of 1μM, 5μM, 10μM and 50μM, respectively, suggesting that elevated concentration of Cu2+ is damaging. Fortunately, CDs are somewhat capable of detoxification, which could enhance the cell viability by the formation of complex between amino groups and Cu2+.

Additionally, we employed Tilapia mossambica for the in vivo detecting of copper since Tilapia mossambica is a kind of food fish, thus it is significant to check the concentration of copper in Tilapia mossambica. Evidently, the bright blue fluorescence was greatly quenched, suggesting that the specific identification of copper could be achieved in living Tilapia mossambic ( Fig. 5).

In summary, fluorescent CDs were fabricated with a facile microwave strategy, which were highly stable and biocompatible. In this work, Cu2+ ions were capable of binding with the amino groups at CDs surface to form complex, leading to the fluorescence quenching of CDs via an electron transfer effect, which supplied a highly sensitive and selective result. Then, CDs were successfully applied to the detection of Cu2+ in living cells and Tilapia mossambica. It has been demonstrated that CDs have the promising applications in inhibiting of the toxicity of copper ions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All authors herein are grateful to the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation Project of China (No. 21405123), Chongqing (cstc2014jcyjA50006) as well as the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2016B039).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.05.035.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
